did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), specifically DCGAN, StyleGAN2, and StyleGAN3-t, to generate synthetic dermatoscopic images of malignant melanoma (MM) lesions for data augmentation. The study trained these deep learning models on a dataset of 1,774 MM images and evaluated the synthetic results using quantitative metrics (FID, KID, precision, recall) and qualitative expert validation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the training of three GAN architectures (DCGAN, StyleGAN2, StyleGAN3-t) on a dataset of 1,774 images, followed by quantitative evaluation (FID, KID, precision, recall) and qualitative validation by a panel of 17 dermatologists. The structure includes standard research sections such as Materials and Methods, Results, and Conclusions, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Advancing synthetic data for dermatology: GAN comparison with multi-metric and expert validation approach
brief_summary: This study addresses data scarcity in malignant melanoma diagnosis by generating synthetic dermatoscopic images using three GAN architectures: DCGAN, StyleGAN2, and StyleGAN3-t. The models were trained on a curated dataset of 1,774 body melanoma images sourced from HAM10000, ISIC 2020, and IdScore_Body databases. Evaluation combined quantitative metrics (FID, KID, precision, recall) with qualitative assessment by a panel of 17 dermatologists. StyleGAN2 achieved the highest image fidelity, while StyleGAN3-t showed stable convergence, both outperforming DCGAN. The results indicate that StyleGAN2-generated images were often indistinguishable from real ones, validating the effectiveness of the proposed multi-metric and expert validation framework for clinical AI applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study exclusively focuses on malignant melanoma (MM) lesions located on the body. A dataset of 1,774 dermatoscopic body MM images was used for training the GAN models. Lesions from the face, palms, and soles were excluded.

all_task_types.target: Synthetic dermatoscopic images of malignant melanoma (MM) lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on generating high-quality synthetic images using three GAN architectures (DCGAN, StyleGAN2, and StyleGAN3-t) for data augmentation. The task is image synthesis, evaluated via quantitative metrics (FID, KID, precision, recall) and qualitative expert validation of image realism and quality.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine)
datasets_used.description: An open-source database of dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions. In this study, it served as a source for body malignant melanoma (MM) images.

use.training: Source of 970 dermatoscopic body MM images used to train the GAN models.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: An open-source database from the ISIC 2020 Challenge. In this study, it served as a source for body malignant melanoma (MM) images.

use.training: Source of 460 dermatoscopic body MM images used to train the GAN models.

datasets_used.name: IdScore_Body
datasets_used.description: A multicentric dataset of dermatoscopic scans. In this study, it provided additional body MM images to complement the open-source datasets.

use.training: Source of 344 additional dermatoscopic body MM images used to train the GAN models.

datasets_used.name: Combined Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: The curated set of 1,774 dermatoscopic body MM images formed by combining HAM10000, ISIC 2020, and IdScore_Body. Lesions from the face, palms, and soles were excluded.

use.training: Used to train the DCGAN, StyleGAN2, and StyleGAN3-t models.
use.other: Primary training data for GAN synthesis

datasets_used.name: Validation Dataset (Expert Assessment)
datasets_used.description: A randomized balanced dataset of 50 images (25 real, 25 synthetic) used for qualitative validation by dermatologists. The real images were randomly selected from the training dataset.

use.testing: Used for qualitative assessment by a panel of 17 dermatologists to evaluate image quality, visual/color realism, and confidence level.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: 7-point Likert scale (for image quality, visual and colour realism, and confidence level)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the fidelity (realism) of synthetic images by comparing feature distributions of real and generated images, assuming a multivariate Gaussian distribution.
To assess the fidelity of synthetic images by measuring the difference between probability distributions using Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) with a polynomial kernel function.

To assess the fidelity and diversity of generated images, respectively, by querying whether generated images fall within the estimated manifold of real images and vice versa using a pre-trained dVGG-16 classifier.
To evaluate the dermatologists' ability to discriminate real from synthetic images, stratified by evaluator expertise and confidence level.
To measure the inter-rater agreement (concordance) of the evaluation on the same image for parameters scored with a 7-point Likert scale.
To assess the inter-rater agreement of the evaluation on parameters scored with the 7-point Likert scale (image quality, visual and colour realism, and confidence level).
To describe the results of the qualitative validation from the dermatologist assessment.
To report the uncertainty for accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity values stratified by evaluator characteristics (e.g., visual realism assessment).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests) for comparing the GAN models or expert validation results. It relied on quantitative metrics (FID, KID, precision, recall) and descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, Fleiss’ Kappa, Krippendorf’s Alpha, 95% Confidence Intervals) for evaluation.

Combined Training Dataset (1,774 body MM.metric: Quantitative GAN Evaluation Metrics (FID, KID, Precision, Recall)
Combined Training Dataset (1,774 body MM.notes: StyleGAN2 achieved the best image fidelity (lowest FID and KID). StyleGAN3-t demonstrated stable but slower convergence. Both StyleGAN models outperformed DCGAN in fidelity. Precision values were comparable across models, while DCGAN had higher recall, suggesting greater diversity but potentially lower fidelity.

Validation Dataset (50 images: 25 real,.metric: Qualitative Expert Validation Metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Fleiss’ Kappa, Krippendorf’s Alpha)
Validation Dataset (50 images: 25 real,.notes: StyleGAN2-generated images were often indistinguishable from real ones, reflected in low specificity and accuracy values among evaluators. Sensitivity was higher for images of medium to high quality and high visual realism. Intra-rater concordance (Fleiss’ Kappa/Krippendorf’s Alpha) was generally very low except for evaluators expressing a high level of confidence (0.53).

architecture_used.main_model: Three GAN architectures: DCGAN (Deep Convolutional GAN), StyleGAN2, and StyleGAN3-t
architecture_used.pretraining: StyleGAN2 and StyleGAN3-t utilized transfer learning (fine-tuned). DCGAN was custom-developed.
architecture_used.input_size: DCGAN: 256x256 pixels; StyleGAN2 and StyleGAN3-t: 512x512 pixels

DCGAN.generator: Transposed convolutions, Batch Normalization, ReLU activation (all layers except output), Tanh activation (output layer)
DCGAN.discriminator: Convolutional layers, Batch Normalization, LeakyReLU activation (all layers except output), Sigmoid activation (output layer), Spectral Normalization, Label Smoothing (real labels changed from 1 to 0.9)
DCGAN.optimization_techniques: Stride convolutions (Discriminator), fractional-stride convolutions (Generator), learning rate schedulers, label smoothing

StyleGAN2.augmentation: Adaptive Discriminator Augmentation (ADA) with probability p=0.7

StyleGAN3-t.modifications: Generator architecture modified to inject noise with a constant learning input and adjust image style in each convolutional layer based on latent code
StyleGAN3-t.augmentation: Adaptive Discriminator Augmentation (ADA) with probability p=0.7

classification_or_output_head.DCGAN: Sigmoid activation for discriminator output; Tanh for generator output
classification_or_output_head.StyleGAN_models: Not explicitly detailed beyond standard GAN discriminator outputs

preprocessing_techniques: Exclusion of lesions from the face, palms, and soles to ensure anatomical consistency
Combination of images from three databases (HAM10000, ISIC 2020, IdScore_Body) into a single training set
Random x-flips (horizontal flipping) for data augmentation
Adaptive Discriminator Augmentation (ADA) with probability p=0.7 for StyleGAN models
Label smoothing (changing real labels from 1 to 0.9) for DCGAN discriminator

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as a standard data augmentation technique for the StyleGAN2 and StyleGAN3-t models during training.

Used for StyleGAN2 and StyleGAN3-t models to dynamically adjust augmentation intensity based on model needs. Applied with a probability p=0.7 to prevent overfitting and mode collapse while maintaining image diversity.
Applied to the DCGAN discriminator. Real image labels were changed from 1 to 0.9 to reduce discriminator confidence and stabilize training.

Generated synthetic dermatoscopic images of malignant melanoma lesions using three GAN architectures (DCGAN, StyleGAN2, StyleGAN3-t) trained on a dataset of 1,774 body MM images to augment the training data.

conclusions: StyleGAN2 achieved the highest image fidelity among the tested architectures, with a Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) of 18.89 and Kernel Inception Distance (KID) of 0.0025.
StyleGAN3-t demonstrated stable but slower convergence compared to StyleGAN2, while DCGAN exhibited significantly higher FID scores and an unstable training trend.
Both StyleGAN models outperformed DCGAN in terms of diversity and fidelity, with StyleGAN2 showing the best overall quantitative performance.

Qualitative validation by a panel of 17 dermatologists indicated that StyleGAN2-generated images were often indistinguishable from real ones, as evidenced by low accuracy and specificity values in the expert assessment.

The study proposes a holistic validation framework that integrates expert assessments with state-of-the-art quantitative metrics to ensure synthetic images are clinically relevant for dermatological AI applications.
Relying solely on precision and recall metrics can lead to misleading conclusions regarding synthetic image quality, highlighting the necessity of employing multiple evaluation metrics.
Intra-rater concordance among dermatologists was generally very low, except for evaluators who expressed a high level of confidence in their assessments (Krippendorf’s Alpha of 0.53).
important_remarks: The study is limited to body malignant melanoma lesions; lesions on the face, palms, and soles were excluded due to distinct dermoscopic patterns, limiting generalizability to other anatomical sites.

The training dataset (1,774 images) was derived from three sources (HAM10000, ISIC 2020, IdScore_Body), potentially introducing dataset bias and limiting the diversity of skin tones and lesion presentations compared to larger global datasets.
Expert validation relied on a small sample size of 50 images (25 real, 25 synthetic) assessed by 17 dermatologists, which may limit the statistical power and robustness of the qualitative findings.

Inter-rater agreement among dermatologists was generally very low (Fleiss’ Kappa 0.183, Krippendorf’s Alpha mostly <0.2), indicating significant subjectivity and inconsistency in expert assessment of synthetic image quality.
StyleGAN2 achieved high fidelity (FID 18.89) but low recall (0.2604), suggesting the model may suffer from mode collapse or limited diversity in the generated images despite high realism.

The study highlights that relying solely on precision and recall metrics can be misleading for evaluating synthetic image quality, necessitating a multi-metric approach including FID, KID, and expert validation.
No formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported for comparing GAN models or expert validation results; evaluation relied on descriptive statistics and confidence intervals.

The synthetic images were generated exclusively for data augmentation purposes; the paper does not report on the downstream impact of these synthetic images on the performance of diagnostic classification models.

Ethical approval was obtained for the use of the IdScore_Body dataset (Approval Code: 16801), while HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 were used as open-access datasets, but specific consent for synthetic data generation and distribution is not explicitly detailed beyond the source licenses.
